design_philosophy.core_loop: Recruit ninja companions by building trust, explore dungeons, engage in turn-based tactical combat, influence provincial warfare through espionage/sabotage, progressively advance toward final confrontation with Oda Nobunaga
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Race against time (1582-1601 deadline) to gather enough power and allies while navigating hazardous dungeons and macro-level warlord politics
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Command a ninja clan on a legendary quest for vengeance against a demonic warlord; build a coalition of diverse companions (ninjas, sages, samurai, wizards) across feudal Japan
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Hybrid sim-strategy RPG that refuses genre purity: demands both tactical dungeon combat AND relationship management AND geopolitical strategy; enforces a strict 19-year campaign timer; makes NPC recruitment central to progression rather than optional; path selection (normal vs magician route) creates asymmetric encounters

difficulty.checkpointing: Dungeons are hazardous with random monster/outlaw encounters; no save-scum protection indicated
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: player chooses dungeon order (18 total, mostly optional), selects which NPCs to recruit, decides whether to engage in provincial warfare, influences difficulty through relationship-building and trust mechanics

progression.upgrade_system: Character recruitment and team composition; NPC stat/ability growth through combat; class diversity includes Ninja, Sage, Wizard, Warrior, Iga/Koga Ninja variants, Sohei, Hermit, Samurai, Ronin, Swordsman, Sorcerer, and others
progression.unlock_mechanism: NPC recruitment locked behind trust threshold (acquired through dialogue at tea houses/inns); optional dungeons playable in any order; mandatory progression requires conquering provinces neighboring Nobunaga's territory; magician vs normal path determines late-game enemy pools; neighboring province conquest gates access to final section
replayability_hooks: Two branching paths (normal/magician) with different enemy encounters
18 dungeons with optional choice in completion order
Multiple NPC recruitment possibilities creates variable team compositions
Provincial conquest mechanics create different strategic landscapes
Rival clan characters refuse recruitment, adding faction-based constraints

world_building_techniques: NPC relationship narratives established through tea house/inn conversations
Environmental storytelling: province control shifts show political landscape changes
Character class diversity reflects feudal Japan social hierarchy
Branching path mechanic (magician vs normal) creates alternate mythological contexts
Villain framing: Oda Nobunaga as demonic warlord threat unifies narrative across geopolitical and personal revenge arcs

boss_design.philosophy: Ultimate confrontation structured as time-gated narrative goal (Oda Nobunaga as final boss, must defeat by 1601 or game over); boss encounters embedded within larger dungeon and strategic conquest framework

faction_indicators: Rival clan affiliation (Iga vs Koga ninja split represented in NPC classes)
Daimyo allegiances (player must build trust with feudal lords to join provincial battles)
Class distinctions (samurai, ronin, sohei, hermit, sorcerer roles create social strata)
NPC recruitment refusals based on clan rivalry signal faction loyalty
danger_signals: Warlord conquest mechanics: Nobunaga's territorial expansion threatens player autonomy
Time pressure: 19-year deadline creates existential threat narrative
Random dungeon encounters with monsters/outlaws indicate lawlessness and danger zones
Espionage/sabotage mechanics against competing daimyo suggest betrayal and coercion
environmental_storytelling: Province control maps show dynamic geopolitical shifts
Hazardous dungeon placement indicates safe vs contested territories
Tea houses and inns serve as social nexuses for trust-building and recruitment
Magician path triggers Western mythological monster encounters (Hellhound, Marchosias), creating parallel world-building
Historical timeline (1582-1601) anchors narrative in sengoku transformation period
